explanation: The formula expresses that, at all times, if 'prop_0' holds, then 'prop_1' must also hold at that time. This is a global safety property.
This says that whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true at that time. In LTL, this is usually expressed as 'globally, if prop_0 then prop_1'.
This is an implication stating that at all times, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at that time. So, globally (G), prop_0 -> prop_1.
The formula expresses that globally, whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at that point. This is done using the implication operator inside a globally (G) operator.

The specification requires that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time (or immediately in response). In LTL, this is captured as a global implication: globally, if prop_0, then prop_1.
The formula encodes that whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at the same time point: globally, prop_0 implies prop_1.
This formula expresses that globally, whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at that time. In LTL, this is formulated as a safety property: G (prop_0 -> prop_1).

This expresses that whenever prop_0 holds at some point in the future, then at the same point, prop_1 must also hold. In LTL, this is formulated as: globally, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must also be true at that moment.
This is a simple implication: whenever 'prop_0' holds, 'prop_1' must also hold. In LTL, the globally (G) operator is used to enforce this condition at all times.
The formula requires that whenever 'prop_0' is true, 'prop_1' must also be true at that time. In LTL, this is usually written as 'Globally, prop_0 implies prop_1'.
